title: English horn

infobox.value: about 1720 from the [oboe da caccia](/wiki/Oboe_da_caccia "Oboe da caccia")

([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ˌkɔːr ˈɒŋɡleɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/- ɑːŋˈɡleɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) or original French: [[kɔʁ ɑ̃ɡlɛ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French");[[3]](#cite_note-3) [plural](/wiki/Plural_noun "Plural noun"): *cors anglais*), or **English horn** (mainly [North America](/wiki/North_American_English "North American English")), is a [double-reed](/wiki/Double_reed "Double reed") [woodwind instrument](/wiki/Woodwind_instrument "Woodwind instrument") in the [oboe](/wiki/Oboe "Oboe") family. It is approximately one and a half times the length of an oboe, making it essentially an **alto oboe** in F.

The **cor anglais** ([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ˌkɔːr ˈɒŋɡleɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/- ɑːŋˈɡleɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) or original French: [[kɔʁ ɑ̃ɡlɛ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French");[[3]](#cite_note-3) [plural](/wiki/Plural_noun "Plural noun"): *cors anglais*), or **English horn** (mainly [North America](/wiki/North_American_English "North American English")), is a [double-reed](/wiki/Double_reed "Double reed") [woodwind instrument](/wiki/Woodwind_instrument "Woodwind instrument") in the [oboe](/wiki/Oboe "Oboe") family. It is approximately one and a half times the length of an oboe, making it essentially an **alto oboe** in F.

The cor anglais is a [transposing instrument](/wiki/Transposing_instrument "Transposing instrument") pitched in [F](/wiki/F_(musical_note) "F (musical note)"), a [perfect fifth](/wiki/Perfect_fifth "Perfect fifth") lower than the [oboe](/wiki/Oboe "Oboe") (a C instrument).[[4]](#cite_note-4) This means that music for the cor anglais is written a perfect fifth higher than the instrument sounds. The fingering and playing technique used for the cor anglais are essentially the same as those of the oboe, and oboists typically double on the cor anglais when required. The cor anglais normally lacks the lowest B♭ key found on most oboes, and so its sounding range stretches from E3 (written B♮) below [middle C](/wiki/Middle_C "Middle C") to C6 two octaves above middle C. Some versions have a Low B♭ key to extend the range down one more note to sounding E♭3.

## Description and timbre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cor_anglais&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description and timbre")]

The pear-shaped bell (called [Liebesfuß](/wiki/Liebesfu%C3%9F "Liebesfuß")) of the cor anglais gives it a more covered [timbre](/wiki/Timbre "Timbre") than the oboe, closer in tonal quality to the [oboe d'amore](/wiki/Oboe_d%27amore "Oboe d'amore"). Whereas the oboe is the soprano instrument of the oboe family, the cor anglais is generally regarded as the [alto](/wiki/Alto "Alto") member of the family, and the oboe d'amore—pitched between the two in the key of A—as the [mezzo-soprano](/wiki/Mezzo-soprano "Mezzo-soprano") member.[[5]](#cite_note-DelMar143-5) The cor anglais is perceived to have a more mellow and plaintive tone than the oboe.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The difference in sound results primarily from a wider reed and a [conical bore](/wiki/Conical_bore "Conical bore") that expands over a greater distance than the oboe's; although darker in tone and lower in pitch than the oboe, its sound is distinct from (though naturally blends with) the sound of the [bassoon](/wiki/Bassoon "Bassoon") family. Its appearance differs from the oboe in that the instrument is notably longer, the reed is attached to a slightly curved metal tube called the [bocal](/wiki/Bocal "Bocal"), or crook, and the bell has a bulbous shape ("Liebesfuß").

The cor anglais is usually notated in the [treble clef](/wiki/Clef#treble_corn "Clef"), a perfect fifth higher than sounding pitch. Alto clef written at sounding pitch is occasionally used, even by as late a composer as [Sergei Prokofiev](/wiki/Sergei_Prokofiev "Sergei Prokofiev"). In late-18th- and early-19th-century Italy, where the instrument was often played by bassoonists instead of oboists, it was notated in the bass clef an octave below sounding pitch (as found in [Rossini's](/wiki/Gioachino_Rossini "Gioachino Rossini") [Overture to *William Tell*](/wiki/William_Tell_Overture "William Tell Overture")). French operatic composers up to [Fromental Halévy](/wiki/Fromental_Hal%C3%A9vy "Fromental Halévy") notated the instrument at sounding pitch in the [mezzo-soprano clef](/wiki/Clef#mezzo-soprano_clef "Clef"), which enabled the player to read the part as if it were in the treble clef.[[5]](#cite_note-DelMar143-5)

Although the instrument usually descends only to (written) low B♮, continental instruments with an extension to low B♭ (sounding E♭) have existed since early in the 19th century.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Examples of works requiring this note (while acknowledging its exceptional nature) include [Arnold Schoenberg](/wiki/Arnold_Schoenberg "Arnold Schoenberg")'s *[Gurre-Lieder](/wiki/Gurre-Lieder "Gurre-Lieder")*, [Gustav Mahler](/wiki/Gustav_Mahler "Gustav Mahler")'s *[Das Lied von der Erde](/wiki/Das_Lied_von_der_Erde "Das Lied von der Erde")*, [Heitor Villa-Lobos](/wiki/Heitor_Villa-Lobos "Heitor Villa-Lobos")'s *[Chôros No. 6](/wiki/Ch%C3%B4ros_No._6 "Chôros No. 6")*, and [Karlheinz Stockhausen](/wiki/Karlheinz_Stockhausen "Karlheinz Stockhausen")'s *[Zeitmaße](/wiki/Zeitma%C3%9Fe "Zeitmaße")*. [Antonín Dvořák](/wiki/Anton%C3%ADn_Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k "Antonín Dvořák"), in his *[Scherzo capriccioso](/wiki/Scherzo_capriccioso_(Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k) "Scherzo capriccioso (Dvořák)")*, even writes for the cor anglais down to low A, though it seems unlikely that such an extension ever existed.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Reeds used to play the cor anglais are similar to those used for an oboe, consisting of a piece of cane folded in two. While the cane on an oboe reed is mounted on a small metal tube (the staple) partially covered in cork, there is no such cork on a cor anglais reed, which fits directly on the bocal. The cane part of the reed is wider and longer than that of the oboe. Unlike American-style oboe reeds, cor anglais reeds typically have some wire at the base, approximately 5 mm (0.20 in) from the top of the string used to attach the cane to the staple. This wire serves to hold the two blades of cane together and stabilize tone and pitch.

Perhaps the best-known makers of modern cors anglais are the French firms of [F. Lorée](/wiki/F._Lor%C3%A9e "F. Lorée"), [Marigaux](/wiki/Marigaux "Marigaux"), and Rigoutat, the British firm of [T. W. Howarth](/wiki/Howarth_of_London "Howarth of London"), and the American firm [Fox Products](/wiki/Fox_Products "Fox Products"). Instruments from smaller makers, such as [A. Laubin](/wiki/A._Laubin "A. Laubin"), are also sought after. Instruments are usually made from [African blackwood](/wiki/African_blackwood "African blackwood") ([aka](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/AKA "wikt:AKA") Grenadilla), although some makers offer instruments in a choice of alternative woods as well, such as [cocobolo](/wiki/Cocobolo "Cocobolo") (Howarth) or violet wood (Lorée), which are said to alter the voice of the cor anglais slightly, producing a more mellow sound. Fox has recently made some instruments in plastic resin and maple, the latter being the wood traditionally used for [bassoons.](/wiki/Bassoon "Bassoon")

## History and etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cor_anglais&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History and etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/English_Horn_ca._1860%E2%80%9370.jpg/250px-English_Horn_ca._1860%E2%80%9370.jpg)](/wiki/File:English_Horn_ca._1860%E2%80%9370.jpg)

English horn, c. 1860–70

The term *cor anglais* is French for *English horn*, but the instrument is neither from England nor related to the various conical-bore brass instruments called "horns", such as the [French horn](/wiki/French_horn "French horn"), the [natural horn](/wiki/Natural_horn "Natural horn"), the [post horn](/wiki/Post_horn "Post horn"), or the [tenor horn](/wiki/Tenor_horn "Tenor horn"). The instrument originated in [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia") about 1720 when a bulb bell was fitted to a curved *[oboe da caccia](/wiki/Oboe_da_caccia "Oboe da caccia")*-type body by the Weigel family of [Breslau](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"). The two-keyed, open-belled, straight tenor oboe (French *[taille de hautbois](/wiki/Taille_(instrument) "Taille (instrument)")*, "tenor oboe"), and more particularly the flare-belled *oboe da caccia*, resembled the horns played by angels in religious images of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). This gave rise in German-speaking central Europe to the [Middle High German](/wiki/Middle_High_German "Middle High German") name *engellisches Horn*, meaning *angelic horn*. Because *engellisch* also meant *English* in the vernacular of the time, the "angelic horn" became the "English horn". In the absence of any better alternative, the curved, bulb-belled tenor oboe then retained the name even after the *oboe da caccia* fell into disuse around 1760.[[9]](#cite_note-angelic-9) The name first appeared regularly in Italian, German, and Austrian scores from 1749 on, usually in the Italian form *corno inglese.*[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The earliest known orchestral part specifically for the instrument is in the Vienna version of [Niccolò Jommelli](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Jommelli "Niccolò Jommelli")'s opera *Ezio* dating from 1749,[[11]](#cite_note-11) where it was given the Italian name *corno inglese*.[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Gluck](/wiki/Christoph_Willibald_Gluck "Christoph Willibald Gluck") and [Haydn](/wiki/Joseph_Haydn "Joseph Haydn") followed suit in the 1750s,[[13]](#cite_note-finkel-13) and the first [English horn concertos](/wiki/English_horn_concerto "English horn concerto") were written in the 1770s. The Schwarzenberg Wind Harmonie of 1771 employed 2 cors anglais as well as 2 oboes, 2 bassoons and 2 horns. Johan Went was first cor anglais and Ignaz Teimer (father of the Teimer brothers) was second cor anglais. The first oboe trios were composed by Johan Went for the Teimer brothers. The oboe and cor anglais writing in these original Bohemian/Viennese trios by Johan Went and Joseph Triebensee are notable as written by oboists for oboists and include some early examples of florid virtuosic writing for the cor anglais, paving the way for similar writing to come. In 1796 Johann and Franz Teimer died. The first recorded performance of an oboe trio was 1793 (which Beethoven attended). While the Teimer brothers performed in Vienna and environs, over 20 oboe trios were composed. Phillip Teimer continued to play the cor anglais in Schikaneder's opera house in Vienna. He also sang some roles with the company due to his sonorous bass voice. Many cor anglais parts were specially written for him by Stengel, Süssmayr, Paer, Winter, Weigl, Eberl, Eybler, Salieri, Hummel, Schacht and Fisher.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Considering the name "cor anglais", it is ironic that the instrument was not regularly used in France before about 1800 or in England before the 1830s.[[13]](#cite_note-finkel-13) It is mentioned in the *[Penny Cyclopedia](/wiki/Penny_Cyclopedia "Penny Cyclopedia")* from 1838 as "The English Horn, or Corno Inglese, is a deeper-toned oboe [...]", while the first identified printed use of the term *cor anglais* in English was in 1870.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In the UK the instrument is colloquially generally referred to as the "cor".[[5]](#cite_note-DelMar143-5) The local equivalent for "English horn" is used in most other European languages, while a few languages use their equivalent of "alto oboe".

Due to the earlier bowed or angular forms it took, the suggestion has been made that *anglais* might be a corruption of [Middle French](/wiki/Middle_French "Middle French") *anglé* (angular, or bent at an angle, *angulaire* in modern French),[[16]](#cite_note-16) but this has been rejected on grounds that there is no evidence of the term *cor anglé* before it was offered as a possible origin of *anglais* in the late 19th century.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Until the 20th century, there were few solo pieces for the instrument with a large ensemble (such as [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra") or [concert band](/wiki/Concert_band "Concert band")). Important examples of such concertos and concertante works are:

* [William Alwyn](/wiki/William_Alwyn "William Alwyn")'s *Autumn Legend* for English horn and string orchestra (1954)
* [Emmanuel Chabrier](/wiki/Emmanuel_Chabrier "Emmanuel Chabrier")'s *Lamento* for English horn and orchestra (1875)
* [Aaron Copland](/wiki/Aaron_Copland "Aaron Copland")'s *[Quiet City](/wiki/Quiet_City_(music) "Quiet City (music)")* for trumpet, English horn, and string orchestra (1940) †
* [Miguel del Aguila](/wiki/Miguel_del_Aguila "Miguel del Aguila"), *Broken Rondo* for solo English horn and orchestra
* [Gaetano Donizetti](/wiki/Gaetano_Donizetti "Gaetano Donizetti")'s Concertino in G major (1816)
* [Arthur Honegger](/wiki/Arthur_Honegger "Arthur Honegger")'s *[Concerto da camera](/wiki/Concerto_da_camera_(Honegger) "Concerto da camera (Honegger)")* for flute, English horn and string orchestra (1948)
* [Gordon Jacob](/wiki/Gordon_Jacob "Gordon Jacob")'s Rhapsody for English horn and strings (1948)
* [Aaron Jay Kernis](/wiki/Aaron_Jay_Kernis "Aaron Jay Kernis")' *Colored Field* (1994)
* [James MacMillan](/wiki/James_MacMillan "James MacMillan")'s *The World's Ransoming*, for obbligato English horn and orchestra (1995–96), part of the orchestral triptych *Triduum* (1995–97) †
* [Walter Piston](/wiki/Walter_Piston "Walter Piston")'s Fantasy for English horn, harp and string orchestra (1952)
* [Ned Rorem](/wiki/Ned_Rorem "Ned Rorem")'s Concerto for English horn and orchestra (1992)
* [Peter Seabourne](/wiki/Peter_Seabourne "Peter Seabourne")'s Concerto for English horn and orchestra (2013)
* [Jean Sibelius](/wiki/Jean_Sibelius "Jean Sibelius")' *[The Swan of Tuonela](/wiki/The_Swan_of_Tuonela "The Swan of Tuonela")* (1893) †
* [Jack Stamp](/wiki/Jack_Stamp "Jack Stamp")'s *Elegy for English horn and Band* (2004)
* [Pēteris Vasks](/wiki/P%C4%93teris_Vasks "Pēteris Vasks")' Concerto for English horn and orchestra (1989)
* [Ermanno Wolf-Ferrari](/wiki/Ermanno_Wolf-Ferrari "Ermanno Wolf-Ferrari")'s Concertino in A♭, op. 34 (1947)

[David Stock](/wiki/David_Stock "David Stock")'s concerto "[Oborama](https://soundcloud.com/david-stock-1/01-david-stock-oborama)" (2010) features the English Horn and its other members as a soloist, the instrument changing in each movement. (ex. Oboe D'amore in movement 3 and Bass Oboe in movement 4)

† Though concertante in nature, these are just orchestral works featuring extensive solos, with the player seated within the orchestra
